Kabamo DigitalDemoDashboardHow it worksRepository

How it works — and why

The problem the demonstrator addresses, the reframe it proposes, and what it is honest about.

The problem it addresses

Bed-net campaigns size demand from a stale census or a population estimate. The real unit of net demand is not headcount — it is sleeping spaces (mattresses). Get the denominator wrong and you over- or under-supply, district by district. The supply chain assumes a live register of demand it rarely actually has.

The reframe: register the demand, keep it live

Make the household the reporter. A short USSD dialogue captures household size, under-5s, pregnancy and — crucially — sleeping spaces, derives a capped net entitlement, and timestamps the record for a ~6-month re-survey. This is a consequent implementation of the established sleeping-space (Senegal) method — not a claim of invention.

Net demand is keyed on sleeping spaces, not people: the adult son on his own mattress is a real net need that a headcount-per-household rule would miss.

How the demo maps to a real process

The five value chains map onto the WHO/MSH management cycle:

  • 1 · Register the demand → Selection & Quantification (the flagship; builds the denominator)
  • 2 · Quantify & allocate → Selection & Quantification
  • 3 · Supply to CHC → Procurement
  • 4 · Last-mile & resistance → Storage & Distribution
  • 5 · Protection & monitoring → Use (which loops back to refresh the register)

The decomposition into five chains is a reasonable cut of the process, not a fact of the case.

USSD floor, smartphone ceiling

USSD is the floor: it works on any feature phone, with no app and no data plan, which is what reaches the unphoned-adjacent majority. A smartphone view over the same data model is the ceiling — richer entry where devices allow — never the minimum requirement.

The net cap is a budget control — not a demand truth

The 4-net household cap is a budget and equity control. At roughly 0.59 nets per person it sits about 18% over the one-net-per-two WHO target — a deliberate buffer. The sleeping-space count is the metric that balances budget, demand, vulnerability, region and transport. Monitoring should continuously challenge the cap rather than treat it as fixed truth.

New household vs. update — the demo

On the Demo page, each fresh run uses a new masked Tanzanian number, so distinct households accumulate. Same household re-dials reuses the last number, so the existing record updates (e.g. a pregnancy now reported) instead of creating a duplicate — demonstrating the continuous, re-surveyed register.

What we are honest about

Coverage bias. Phone self-report under-counts the unphoned poorest. Mitigation: CHW-assisted entry. USSD is the default channel, not the only one.
Data protection. A household configuration against a (hashed) phone number is sensitive personal data. A real deployment needs a named data custodian, consent and purpose limitation (e.g. Tanzania PDPA 2022, GDPR-derived obligations). The demo uses synthetic / consented data only.
Not an outbreak tool. This is for planned, recurring supply to a known population (ITNs, ORS/zinc, vitamin A, ARV/TB continuity, maternal-neonatal). Acute outbreak response is a different problem — deliberately excluded.
Reachability. The sandbox code only works in the aggregator's web simulator. Real handsets need a paid live short code in a covered country (Tanzania: Vodacom/Airtel/Tigo — feasible). Not reachable on Swisscom (no aggregator interconnect).

Try it

Open the Demo, pick a value chain, and press Run process — each step is a real exchange with the live ussd.php. Registrations show up on the Dashboard within about 15 seconds.

Kabamo Digital — USSD demand-register demonstrator · Apache-2.0 · © 2026 Urs Gehrig · GitHub
Method demonstrator, not a product. Synthetic / consented data only. Region views show counts, never real locations.
